title: Lesson 444
transcript: All right, back in domain number one, you'll remember, which seems like weeks ago, right? It might be weeks ago for you, but we did talk about as part of security management, the importance of security focusing on and supporting business continuity management. And we talked about back there in domain number one about the importance of supporting things like doing the BIA and also understanding external dependencies that the organization may be relying on. Because obviously, here we're focusing on business continuity management. and really the steps that are needed by an organization in order to prepare for any sort of negative eventuality that may put the organization at risk and be able to survive it. I mean ultimately that's what business continuity planning is all about and business continuity management keyword management obviously ensures that once we have ways to deal with disasters, business continuity plans, disaster recovery plans, and so on, they are effective, but we are also thinking about the security aspects of business continuity management. Just because an organization is going through a disaster doesn't mean that they can forget about protecting their most valuable assets like people, corporate reputation, And also it becomes very important and maybe even more important in the event of a disaster for the organization to achieve things like compliance to laws and regulations and privacy commitments and so on. The organization can't really say, well, we're going through a disaster, and so we can't meet the requirements for protecting data and privacy laws and the privacy of our clients and customers and so on. In fact, in the event of a disaster. may be even more important for the organization to be able to do that. And so it's really important to involve security as part of business continuity management right from the start and throughout each of the phases of coming up with plans. So that's what we're going to focus on here in this section. And really what we're going to present here is the industry. And again, this is not what ISE 2 comes up with. Anything that obviously is addressed on the exam comes from First of all, the exam outline, which is derived from the common body of knowledge of security, which comes from the industry. So here we're going to talk about the steps involved in really being prepared for any sort of negative eventuality that companies will refer to as a disaster. So business continuity management, in other words. And just like everything, the first thing that you need is a policy. Remember, a policy is a corporate law that really communicates management's intentions to the entire organization and stakeholders, for example. you know what's important and obviously in this case the importance of business continuity management is something that needs to be communicated to all and the entire organization itself. And that's why organizations need a business continuity management policy that talks about accountability, responsibility, why we have it and whose roles are involved and so on, and the role of security within that too, right? So policy is the foundation for anything that obviously has value to the organization that security has to be able to support, right? Now, I think we talked about the next step in business continuity management being the BIA. I think we're going to talk about the BIA and what's involved. But ultimately, the BIA is a process by which the organization can understand what is truly critical and essential. and also how critical and essential each of those things are. So a BIA is ultimately a walk through your entire organization to understand which processes, functions, services, applications, network segments, systems, technologies, architectures, assets, I think I said assets, but are actually critical and essential and to what degree they are critical and essential. In fact, you'll learn and you'll have to remember that the output of a BIA is or are time parameters like NTD and RTO and RPO and so on. Those are important for us to remember, but those time parameters really portray the criticality and essentiality of all kinds of things like products, services, applications, relationships, intellectual property, systems, architectures, technologies, and so on. At the end of the BIA, once that's been completed, you'll know what is your most critical thing and the next most critical and the next most critical based on these time parameters that are really the output of the BIA itself. Really important to remember that in order to do a proper BIA, which is essentially the first step in being able to be prepared, for disasters and allow the organization to survive, you need to involve people that know the business. Because you can take people that know the business and teach them how to do, for example, business continuity management, including how to do a BIA. But you can't take people that know how to do business continuity management including a BIA, and teach them the business. So you've got to start with stakeholders that understand the business. And that involves security as well. Security has to be part of this so that we can understand criticality from the perspective of security. Once you understand what's truly critical and essential and how important they are to the organization and its stakeholders, it's important to address those, right? Once you know your most critical function, for example, You obviously want to try and prevent as much as you can any sort of disasters or negative things that can impact that critical function or process, whatever it is. So that's what step number three is. You put in a preventive type of action. controls that will prevent bad things from happening. And then of course you can't prevent everything. And so the next step is to come up with strategies and contingency plans. that will allow you to deal with negative eventualities that are referred to as obviously disasters. All right, so this is where you come up with business continuity plans, disaster recovery plans, basically to match and meet those time parameters that you found and are the output of the BIA itself. And then once you have plans in place, we're going to talk more about this, obviously those plans need to be tested and they need to be updated on a regular basis. And those people that have responsibilities according to those plans, We'll need to obviously have the proper skills to be able to execute the responsibilities as part of those plans. So this is why we call it business continuity management. Once you have plans in place, you're not finished. Those plans need to be constantly updated. And if there's any sort of significant change in the organization itself, it needs to be reflected in your business continuity plans and disaster recovery plans. And again, people on a regular basis need to be able to understand what they need to do in the event of a real disaster. So drills and exercises and, of course, testing of the plans is something that ultimately needs to be done on a regular basis. And again, the theme here is that security needs to be involved in all of this from the start and throughout and the ongoing. management of your business continuity plans, disaster recovery plans. And that's why we call it business continuity management. Those things need to be managed on a regular basis. And that's the maintenance part of it as well.
